author: @stevetobias4890
text: Good work, just make sure that you let the heat do the work and don't put too much force on the blade. Also careful to use a soldering iron like you used in the video, some have the thermal power switch much closer to the tip of the iron and are not suitable for this application.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey

author: @ericvonessex9353
text: Many many thanks, what a brilliant idea and you have kindly solved a problem I've had! I have a large sheet of acrylic glass that's several decades old and somewhat brittle possibly due to age. My intention has been to saw it into 15 small glass panels to fit into an internal door that I was given, minus the glass! Using a conventional hand saw or hacksaw I feared it would crack the acrylic. Using an electric multi-tool saw could also crack it. Using your idea after first applying a hairdryer over the surface of the acrylic seems the safest way of doing the job. Again many thanks for an excellent idea!

replies.author: @ucanleaveyourhaton
replies.text: Great idea - the hairdryer ! Have you tried one of those industrial dryers, that painters use to dry off their paint jobs quickly ? Just like a regular hair dryer, but far hotter. 👍

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Not bad.

I've done something simular in the past involving a glow wire to cut styrofoam. That can be extremely usefull. Pick a piece of hardened wire ( these tend to have a higher resistance, leading to more heat ). One single wire taken out of a bycicle breaking cable would be good. Stretch it, span it across 2 fixed points or maybe put a weight on it on the bottom of the thing hanging from the ceiling. Then take a good power supply and send 2 or three amps through.  Then push a piece of  styrofoam on to it. It'll cut in seconds any shape you want.

Good idea! I'll have to give it a go.

replies.author: @BramleyProductions
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey Thanks, Luke I also did a TV/Radio repair apprenticeship way back in the 1970s my old boss was always telling us off for striping plastic cable and coax cable with pliers or wire cutters or even worse a blunt Stanley type knife! He said the only way is to use a hot knife or old soldering iron to melt the outer plastic cover of stranded copper wire so as not to break or cut into the delicate strands! So another use maybe to strip wire. A very well thought out build for sure well done. Kev.
